original_abstract: We demonstrate that the redox-active metal-organic framework Fe2(dobpdc) (dobpdc4– = 4,4'-dioxidobiphenyl-3,3'-dicarboxylate) undergoes a topotactic oxidative insertion reaction with various weakly coordinating anions, such as BF4– and PF6–. This process occurs with only a minimal contraction of the crystal lattice and triggers the emergence of a broad intervalence charge-transfer band, signaling high electronic mobility within the framework. While the system can undergo both metal- and ligand-based oxidations, we found that only the metal-centered reactions are fully reversible and proceed with high stoichiometric precision under both chemical and electrochemical control. Investigation into the effects of nanoconfinement during these insertion reactions revealed a significant dependence on both the size of the anion and the choice of solvent. Leveraging this reversible anion insertion behavior, we integrated Fe2(dobpdc) into a prototype dual-ion battery featuring a sodium-metal anode. When utilized as a cathode, the framework exhibits an exceptionally high initial reduction potential and maintains stability over at least 50 charge/discharge cycles, achieving a maximum specific energy of 316 Wh/kg.
humanized_abstract: We show here that the redox-active metal-organic framework (MOF) Fe2(dobpdc) (dobpdc4– = 4,4‘-dioxidobiphenyl-3,3‘-dicarboxylate) can be topotactically oxidatively inserted with a variety of weakly coordinating counter-anions such as BF4– and PF6– with only a small lattice contraction and the development of a broad IVCT band suggesting high conductivity in the material. The MOF can be both metallic and ligand-based reduced but the metal-centered insertions are reversible with high stoichiometry under both solution and electrochemical conditions. We also investigated nanoconfinement effects of counter-anions and different solvents during anion insertion and found that counter-anion size and solvent had a significant impact. Based on these results we demonstrate the use of the redox-active MOF, Fe2(dobpdc), as the cathode material in a prototype dual-ion battery (DIB) with a sodium-metal anode). The material shows a large initial reduction potential and is cycled for at least 50 cycles with a maximum specific energy of 316 Wh/kg.

document.output: We show here that the redox-active metal-organic framework (MOF) Fe2(dobpdc) (dobpdc4– = 4,4‘-dioxidobiphenyl-3,3‘-dicarboxylate) can be topotactically oxidatively inserted with a variety of weakly coordinating counter-anions such as BF4– and PF6– with only a small lattice contraction and the development of a broad IVCT band suggesting high conductivity in the material. The MOF can be both metallic and ligand-based reduced but the metal-centered insertions are reversible with high stoichiometry under both solution and electrochemical conditions. We also investigated nanoconfinement effects of counter-anions and different solvents during anion insertion and found that counter-anion size and solvent had a significant impact. Based on these results we demonstrate the use of the redox-active MOF, Fe2(dobpdc), as the cathode material in a prototype dual-ion battery (DIB) with a sodium-metal anode). The material shows a large initial reduction potential and is cycled for at least 50 cycles with a maximum specific energy of 316 Wh/kg.
document.input: We demonstrate that the redox-active metal-organic framework Fe2(dobpdc) (dobpdc4– = 4,4'-dioxidobiphenyl-3,3'-dicarboxylate) undergoes a topotactic oxidative insertion reaction with various weakly coordinating anions, such as BF4– and PF6–. This process occurs with only a minimal contraction of the crystal lattice and triggers the emergence of a broad intervalence charge-transfer band, signaling high electronic mobility within the framework. While the system can undergo both metal- and ligand-based oxidations, we found that only the metal-centered reactions are fully reversible and proceed with high stoichiometric precision under both chemical and electrochemical control. Investigation into the effects of nanoconfinement during these insertion reactions revealed a significant dependence on both the size of the anion and the choice of solvent. When utilized as a cathode, the framework exhibits an exceptionally high initial reduction potential and maintains stability over at least 50 charge/discharge cycles, achieving a maximum specific energy of 316 Wh/kg.
